Name: information_theoretic_neural_pruning
Title: Information-Theoretic Neural Pruning: A Rate-Distortion Approach to Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that neural network pruning can be formulated as a rate-distortion problem, where the goal is to minimize the number of bits required to represent the model while maintaining its predictive performance. By leveraging information-theoretic tools such as mutual information and entropy, we can develop a principled approach to pruning that optimally balances model complexity and accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation have explored the use of geometric and information-theoretic techniques for model compression and pruning. However, these approaches focus primarily on empirical heuristics and lack a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. Our proposal builds upon these works by developing a principled, information-theoretic framework for neural pruning that is grounded in rate-distortion theory.
Abstract: Neural network pruning is a widely used technique for model compression, but existing methods often rely on empirical heuristics and lack a rigorous theoretical foundation. In this proposal, we develop an information-theoretic approach to neural pruning, formulating the problem as a rate-distortion tradeoff between model complexity and accuracy. We leverage tools from information theory, such as mutual information and entropy, to develop a principled pruning framework that optimally balances model complexity and accuracy. Our approach provides a transparent and reproducible method for model compression, with a clear understanding of the tradeoffs involved. We evaluate our approach on a range of benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness in achieving a superior tradeoff between model size and accuracy.
Experiments: Evaluate the performance of our information-theoretic pruning approach on a range of benchmarks, including image classification and language modeling tasks.
Compare our approach to existing pruning methods, including empirical heuristics and geometric techniques.
Investigate the effect of different distortion measures and rate-distortion tradeoffs on the performance of our pruning approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The development of a rigorous information-theoretic framework for neural pruning may require significant theoretical advances in rate-distortion theory and its application to neural networks.
The computational complexity of our approach may be higher than existing empirical heuristics, potentially limiting its applicability to very large models.
The evaluation of our approach may require significant computational resources and large-scale datasets.
